The tumor microenvironment consists of a mix of tumor cells and different types of tumor-infiltrating immune cells. In his lab at the University of California, San Francisco, Robert Blelloch studies tumor immunology.
Li Wang and Hannah Driks, Blelloch’s lab.
Inspired by a student’s idea and emerging evidence of the presence of microRNAs in the plasma of patients with cancer, Blelloch examined whether specific microRNAs in the blood could serve as diagnostic and prognostic markers in people with prostate cancer. 10
10
By developing a method that enabled the detection of even small amounts of microRNAs, his team found that specific microRNAs in the serum were associated with prostate cancer progression. What’s more, adding these small RNAs into the Cancer of the Prostate Risk Assessment (CAPRA) model improved cancer risk prediction, supporting the use of these molecules in clinical settings. 6,7
6,7
The discovery of microRNAs circulating in the blood led Blelloch and his team to question how exactly these molecules moved inside the human body. One possibility was that extracellular vesicles , particularly in the form of exosomes, carried them around. “Vesicles are basically lipid packages, and they can transfer material from one cell to another,” Blelloch explained. “Does that mean microRNAs are being transferred from one cell to another in the blood?”
extracellular vesicles
Blelloch’s team set out to investigate this question, but it turned out to be a fruitless pursuit. “After three years it was kind of a failure,” he recalled. “[The] poor graduate student working on it was looking for evidence of microRNA transfer via these vesicles, and at least in our hands, we were seeing very little evidence for significant transfer.”
The setback sent the researchers back to the drawing board, where they started rethinking the possible roles these exosomes might have.
Long-Distance Control of the Anti-Tumor Immune Response
Exosomes derive from the plasma membrane of cells, meaning they are covered with insoluble proteins that can act as molecular signals, even over long distances.
This idea prompted Blelloch and his team to shift their focus from microRNAs to proteins. They focused on the programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-L1), a molecule known to suppress the anti-tumor immune response, Blelloch explained. Using genetic knockouts of key enzymes involved in exosome biosynthesis, the researchers showed that tumors secrete PD-L1-packed exosomes that travel away from the tumor microenvironment to the lymph nodes, where PD-L1 inhibits T cell activity. 8 Blelloch emphasized that advanced gene-editing technologies, such as CRISPR, were imperative for bringing this work to life. “[In] the paper, everything's CRISPR-ed out. I could've never done that in 2012. No way,” he said.
8

While their findings suggested that PD-L1 plays a key role in exosome-mediated immunosuppression, Blelloch explained that some results indicated that the protein does not act alone. Using mass spectrometry in the tumor-derived exosomes, his team recently identified the poliovirus receptor-related 2 (PVRL2) molecule, a protein believed to act as an immune checkpoint protein, as another suppressor of the anti-tumor immune response. 11 Blelloch and his team are further exploring PVRL2’s role in cancer immunology.
11
By letting discoveries guide his research, Blelloch’s scientific journey reveals how researchers can chase the most exciting results while making significant contributions to their fields. “The fun of being an academic is just let things flow; let things take you where they take you and hope for the best,” he said. “The downside of this kind of career is [that] it’s very risk-taking [and] very insecure. You get beaten down constantly, but boy, there is a lot of joy.”

articles.title: Researchers Reflect on the Lasting Impact of Science’s Landmark Discoveries
articles.text:
Skip to main content Article Researchers Reflect on the Lasting Impact of Science’s Landmark Discoveries From PCR and CRISPR to autophagy and targeted cancer therapies, scientists share the discoveries that shaped their work and continue to influence the future of research. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . May 2, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Researchers share landmark discoveries that inspire them. Image credit: © iStock.com , Natalya Kosarevich PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share S cience is a series of milestones, from fundamental discoveries to innovative technologies. Each one acts like a building block, supporting the next breakthrough and paving the way for future generations of researchers. To explore the personal impact of these milestones, we asked our readers: Which landmark scientific discovery or invention do you find most inspiring, and why? Cell Biology: From Autophagy to Immortal Cells Kaamini Dhanabalan is a bioengineer and molecular imaging scientist. Kaamini Dhanabalan Kaamini Dhanabalan , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara I find the discovery of autophagy deeply inspiring. The idea that cells have an internal recycling system, one that breaks down damaged components and repurposes them for survival, completely changed how we think about health and aging. It shifted the narrative from cells passively accumulating damage to cells actively maintaining themselves. The significance of this work was recognized globally when the 2016 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for uncovering the mechanisms of autophagy, which underscored how a fundamental cellular process can reshape medicine. What fascinates me most is how central autophagy is across biological processes and diseases like neurodegeneration, cancer, immunity, metabolism, and aging. During my PhD, studying autophagy closely showed me how tightly regulated and context-dependent it is. Now seeing intermittent fasting enter mainstream conversations as a way to stimulate autophagy is striking. A molecular pathway once limited to research labs is now influencing everyday health decisions and remains my exact motivation to bridge benches and beds. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Khyati Raina’s work combines stem cell biology and organoid technology. Khyati Raina Khyati Raina , a postdoctoral fellow at Albert Einstein College of Medicine Two discoveries have really shaped how I think about science: the development of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. The idea behind iPSCs is fascinating. It’s almost like reversing time at the cellular level. Scientists can take a fully developed cell, like a skin fibroblast, and reprogram it back into a pluripotent state by changing the set of proteins it expresses. From there, it has the potential to become almost any cell type in the body. The fact that you can generate cells so closely related to an individual, starting from their own tissue, completely changed how we study development and disease. CRISPR-Cas9, on the other hand, feels like having a highly precise molecular tool. It allows us to target and edit specific regions of DNA with remarkable accuracy. Together, these technologies have transformed research across many fields and continue to open up possibilities that once seemed out of reach. Molecular Biology: Amplifying and Silencing Genes Aditya Gupta is a parasitologist at USDA-ARS. Aditya Gupta Aditya Gupta , a postdoctoral fellow at the US Department of Agriculture-Agricultural Research Service (USDA-ARS) The invention of the polymerase chain reaction ( PCR ) by Kary Mullis is particularly inspiring to me. I read this story during my doctoral research, and I still remember how he ran back to his lab to check the PCR results. PCR transformed biology by allowing researchers to amplify tiny amounts of DNA and detect organisms that would otherwise remain hidden. In my work, PCR-based methods are essential for identifying parasites in meat samples and studying their genetic diversity. This technology illustrates how a single innovation can revolutionize multiple fields, from diagnostics to evolutionary biology, and enable discoveries that directly impact public health and disease surveillance. Kasturika Shankar is a biochemist and biophysicist who studies host-pathogen interactions. Kasturika Shankar Kasturika Shankar , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Buffalo The discovery of RNA interference, the finding that cells have a built-in system for silencing specific genes using small RNA molecules. Researchers stumbled upon it almost by accident, and yet it revolutionized both our understanding of gene regulation and gave scientists a powerful tool with which to study and potentially treat disease. It's a reminder that curiosity-driven, fundamental research can lead somewhere transformative in ways nobody initially anticipated. Health Science: From Precision Oncology to Harm Reduction Neil Carleton studies the tumor biology of age-related breast cancers. Neil Carleton Neil Carleton , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Pittsburgh The field of breast cancer research has been particularly inspiring to follow, both historically and in the modern era of precision oncology. The discovery of the estrogen receptor and the development of tamoxifen were among the earliest and most influential examples of targeted cancer therapy. These advances laid the groundwork for an entire field focused on endocrine-targeted treatments. Similarly, the identification of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) and the development of trastuzumab dramatically transformed outcomes for patients with HER2-positive breast cancer. Looking back at these milestones has been especially exciting, and they continue to inform how we think about precision medicine today. Mia Rough is a behavioral neuropharmacologist at Wake Forest University School of Medicine. Mia Rough Mia Rough , a postdoctoral fellow at Wake Forest University School of Medicine One of the developments I find most inspiring is the emergence of harm reduction tools such as naloxone (Narcan) and fentanyl test strips. What inspires me about these advances is that they reflect a public health approach grounded in compassion and evidence, where the immediate goal is to save lives. Harm reduction recognizes that people deserve care and protection regardless of where they are in recovery. To me, some of the most important advances in substance use science are the ones that reduce suffering in the present while also creating opportunities for better long-term outcomes. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage. Related Topics Cell & Molecular Biology , CRISPR , drug discovery , drugs , PCR , Public Health , technology Share You might also be interested in... Article Understanding Long-Read cDNA Sequencing for Rare Genetic Disease Long-read sequencing addresses the limitations of short-read RNA-seq and can capture isoform-specific changes. Share Short-read RNA-seq provides robust gene-level expression but is limited in resolving full-length transcripts and splice variants. Long-read sequencing addresses these limitations by capturing complete isoforms, enabling direct interrogation of transcript structure and isoform-specific changes. This information can identify biomarkers and support therapeutic development. Download this case study to learn more about how full-length transcript sequencing can detect treatment-associated splice variants and characterize transcriptome-wide expression changes. Sponsored by Top Image Credit: ©iStock, filo You might also be interested in... Interview Postdoc Portrait: Kaustav Gangopadhyay By studying the conformational dynamics of kinases, one postdoc reveals how tiny structural changes govern major cellular responses. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Nov 17, 2025 | 2 min read Save for Later This postdoc studies how tiny shifts in kinase structure steer the cell’s larger responses. Image credit: St. Jude Children's research Hospital, ©iStock, selvanegra PDF version Share Q | Write a brief introduction to yourself including the lab you work in and your research background. I am Kaustav Gangopadhyay , a postdoctoral research associate in structural biology at St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital with Dr. Babis Kalodimos. My research explores understanding the conformational dynamics of kinases. I earned my PhD in Biological Sciences from IISER Kolkata, where my work centered on the T cell kinase ZAP-70 and its role in antigen discrimination through kinetic proofreading. Q | How did you first get interested in science and/or your field of research? My interest in science began early with a fascination for how invisible processes shape the visible world around us. I was particularly intrigued by the question of how cells make decisions with such precision, and how mistakes in these decisions can lead to disease. This curiosity grew into a passion for molecular biology and biochemistry during my undergraduate training, where I realized that structural biology offers a unique window into life at atomic detail—allowing us to see not just what proteins do, but how they do it. Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite research directions has been exploring the structural and mechanistic regulation of kinases, enzymes that act as central hubs in cellular signaling. What excites me about studying kinases is their dual nature—they are both highly conserved in their catalytic machinery and incredibly diverse in their regulatory strategies. This balance makes them ideal systems to probe fundamental principles of allostery, cooperativity, and signaling fidelity. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe During my PhD, I focused on the immune kinase ZAP-70, dissecting how its tandem SH2 domains enforce kinetic proofreading in T cells, ensuring discrimination between self and non-self-antigens. Now, in my postdoctoral work, I try to understand the conformational dynamics of kinases owing to understand the toggling of states between inactive and active conformation. Q | What do you find most exciting about your research project? The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been the moments when abstract questions about cellular behavior collapse into clear, testable molecular stories. There’s a special thrill in collecting noisy biophysical data, watching peaks move on an NMR spectrum or a kinetic trace change shape, and then realizing those signals map onto a concrete structural mechanism that explains how a kinase or receptor actually works. Equally exciting has been the collaborative and mentoring side of science: designing experiments with colleagues across disciplines, training juniors to think critically about controls and interpretation, and watching them grow into independent scientists. Finally, the translational edge—knowing that mechanistic insight can point toward new ways to target disease—gives the work a real sense of purpose. Put simply: turning “what if?” into “here’s how” has been the most exhilarating part of my career so far. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I’d be an NMR spectrometer—quiet, patient, and obsessed with tiny motions. It listens to the whispers of atoms, reveals hidden conformations and dynamics, and turns noisy, mysterious signals into stories about how proteins breathe, bind, and switch. It’s perfect for someone who delights in seeing the unseen: sensitive to subtle allostery, versatile across systems, and endlessly curious. Are you a researcher who would like to be featured in the “Postdoc Portraits” series? Send in your application here . Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage. Related Topics Biochemistry , Cell & Molecular Biology , postdoc portraits , postdocs Share May 2026 Genome Music: Rare Disease Sequences Turn Into Songs Scientists and composers bring attention to rare genetic diseases through the power of music. View this Issue Research Resources Podcasts Webinars Videos Infographics eBooks Strategies for Building Scalable Gene Editing Programs Effective gene editing programs rely on a platform-based approach that prioritizes clinical precision, regulatory-compliant data, and manufacturing scalability from the start. Validation and Analysis of 12,000 AI-Driven CAR T Designs in the Bits to Binders Competition Examine how large-scale CAR T-cell functional validation informs the efficacy of AI-driven protein binder design methodologies. Designing Translational CRISPR Genome Editing A platform approach to gene editing brings together design, manufacturing, validation, analytics, and regulatory considerations right from the start. Automating Large-Scale Plasmid DNA Purification In this webinar, Lee Hoang will examine automated strategies for improving scalability and consistency in large-scale plasmid DNA purification. Products Product News Thermo Scientific™ Multiskan™ Ease Absorbance Microplate Reader Absorbance, redefined. Multiskan™ Ease delivers fast, reproducible results with minimal training. LED optics remove lamp changes, while cloud-enabled SkanIt™ software simplifies setup and analysis for reliable ELISA workflows. 3dGRO Human iPSC Derived Liver Organoid Progenitors The 3dGRO Human iPSC Derived Liver Organoid Progenitor express key liver progenitor markers HNF4a, SOX17, FOXA2 and SOX9, and showed proper apical and basal-lateral polarization marked by E-cadherin and ZO1 expression. Gibco CTS Compleo Fill and Finish System – Automated Formulation and Filling to Help Streamline Cell Therapy Manufacturing The CTS Compleo system combines a closed, automated design with intuitive software to streamline formulation and filling while maintaining aseptic integrity and helping reduce risk in cell therapy manufacturing workflows. New CellCelector CLD takes you from thousands of candidates to the top clone, faster Rapid preselection of high-producing clones from candidate pools, saving resources We’ve updated our Privacy Policy to make it clearer how we use your personal data. Please read our Cookie Policy to learn how we use cookies to provide you with a better experience. Khyati Raina Khyati Raina , a postdoctoral fellow at Albert Einstein College of Medicine Two discoveries have really shaped how I think about science: the development of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. The idea behind iPSCs is fascinating. It’s almost like reversing time at the cellular level. Scientists can take a fully developed cell, like a skin fibroblast, and reprogram it back into a pluripotent state by changing the set of proteins it expresses. From there, it has the potential to become almost any cell type in the body. The fact that you can generate cells so closely related to an individual, starting from their own tissue, completely changed how we study development and disease. CRISPR-Cas9, on the other hand, feels like having a highly precise molecular tool. It allows us to target and edit specific regions of DNA with remarkable accuracy. Together, these technologies have transformed research across many fields and continue to open up possibilities that once seemed out of reach. Molecular Biology: Amplifying and Silencing Genes Aditya Gupta is a parasitologist at USDA-ARS. Aditya Gupta Aditya Gupta , a postdoctoral fellow at the US Department of Agriculture-Agricultural Research Service (USDA-ARS) The invention of the polymerase chain reaction ( PCR ) by Kary Mullis is particularly inspiring to me. I read this story during my doctoral research, and I still remember how he ran back to his lab to check the PCR results. PCR transformed biology by allowing researchers to amplify tiny amounts of DNA and detect organisms that would otherwise remain hidden. In my work, PCR-based methods are essential for identifying parasites in meat samples and studying their genetic diversity. This technology illustrates how a single innovation can revolutionize multiple fields, from diagnostics to evolutionary biology, and enable discoveries that directly impact public health and disease surveillance. S cience is a series of milestones, from fundamental discoveries to innovative technologies. Each one acts like a building block, supporting the next breakthrough and paving the way for future generations of researchers. To explore the personal impact of these milestones, we asked our readers: Which landmark scientific discovery or invention do you find most inspiring, and why? Cell Biology: From Autophagy to Immortal Cells Kaamini Dhanabalan is a bioengineer and molecular imaging scientist. Kaamini Dhanabalan Kaamini Dhanabalan , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara I find the discovery of autophagy deeply inspiring. The idea that cells have an internal recycling system, one that breaks down damaged components and repurposes them for survival, completely changed how we think about health and aging. It shifted the narrative from cells passively accumulating damage to cells actively maintaining themselves. The significance of this work was recognized globally when the 2016 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for uncovering the mechanisms of autophagy, which underscored how a fundamental cellular process can reshape medicine. What fascinates me most is how central autophagy is across biological processes and diseases like neurodegeneration, cancer, immunity, metabolism, and aging. During my PhD, studying autophagy closely showed me how tightly regulated and context-dependent it is. Now seeing intermittent fasting enter mainstream conversations as a way to stimulate autophagy is striking. Share Short-read RNA-seq provides robust gene-level expression but is limited in resolving full-length transcripts and splice variants. Long-read sequencing addresses these limitations by capturing complete isoforms, enabling direct interrogation of transcript structure and isoform-specific changes. Interview Postdoc Portrait: Kaustav Gangopadhyay By studying the conformational dynamics of kinases, one postdoc reveals how tiny structural changes govern major cellular responses. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Nov 17, 2025 | 2 min read Save for Later This postdoc studies how tiny shifts in kinase structure steer the cell’s larger responses. Image credit: St. Jude Children's research Hospital, ©iStock, selvanegra PDF version Share Q | Write a brief introduction to yourself including the lab you work in and your research background. I am Kaustav Gangopadhyay , a postdoctoral research associate in structural biology at St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital with Dr. Babis Kalodimos. My research explores understanding the conformational dynamics of kinases. I earned my PhD in Biological Sciences from IISER Kolkata, where my work centered on the T cell kinase ZAP-70 and its role in antigen discrimination through kinetic proofreading. Q | How did you first get interested in science and/or your field of research? My interest in science began early with a fascination for how invisible processes shape the visible world around us. I was particularly intrigued by the question of how cells make decisions with such precision, and how mistakes in these decisions can lead to disease. This curiosity grew into a passion for molecular biology and biochemistry during my undergraduate training, where I realized that structural biology offers a unique window into life at atomic detail—allowing us to see not just what proteins do, but how they do it. Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite research directions has been exploring the structural and mechanistic regulation of kinases, enzymes that act as central hubs in cellular signaling. What excites me about studying kinases is their dual nature—they are both highly conserved in their catalytic machinery and incredibly diverse in their regulatory strategies. This balance makes them ideal systems to probe fundamental principles of allostery, cooperativity, and signaling fidelity. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe During my PhD, I focused on the immune kinase ZAP-70, dissecting how its tandem SH2 domains enforce kinetic proofreading in T cells, ensuring discrimination between self and non-self-antigens. Now, in my postdoctoral work, I try to understand the conformational dynamics of kinases owing to understand the toggling of states between inactive and active conformation. Q | What do you find most exciting about your research project? The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been the moments when abstract questions about cellular behavior collapse into clear, testable molecular stories. There’s a special thrill in collecting noisy biophysical data, watching peaks move on an NMR spectrum or a kinetic trace change shape, and then realizing those signals map onto a concrete structural mechanism that explains how a kinase or receptor actually works. Equally exciting has been the collaborative and mentoring side of science: designing experiments with colleagues across disciplines, training juniors to think critically about controls and interpretation, and watching them grow into independent scientists. Finally, the translational edge—knowing that mechanistic insight can point toward new ways to target disease—gives the work a real sense of purpose. Put simply: turning “what if?” into “here’s how” has been the most exhilarating part of my career so far. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I’d be an NMR spectrometer—quiet, patient, and obsessed with tiny motions. It listens to the whispers of atoms, reveals hidden conformations and dynamics, and turns noisy, mysterious signals into stories about how proteins breathe, bind, and switch. It’s perfect for someone who delights in seeing the unseen: sensitive to subtle allostery, versatile across systems, and endlessly curious. What excites me about studying kinases is their dual nature—they are both highly conserved in their catalytic machinery and incredibly diverse in their regulatory strategies. This balance makes them ideal systems to probe fundamental principles of allostery, cooperativity, and signaling fidelity.

Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates:
Cell Biology
During my PhD, I focused on the immune kinase ZAP-70, dissecting how its tandem SH2 domains enforce kinetic proofreading in T cells, ensuring discrimination between self and non-self-antigens. Now, in my postdoctoral work, I try to understand the conformational dynamics of kinases owing to understand the toggling of states between inactive and active conformation.
Q | What do you find most exciting about your research project?
The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been the moments when abstract questions about cellular behavior collapse into clear, testable molecular stories. There’s a special thrill in collecting noisy biophysical data, watching peaks move on an NMR spectrum or a kinetic trace change shape, and then realizing those signals map onto a concrete structural mechanism that explains how a kinase or receptor actually works.
Equally exciting has been the collaborative and mentoring side of science: designing experiments with colleagues across disciplines, training juniors to think critically about controls and interpretation, and watching them grow into independent scientists. Finally, the translational edge—knowing that mechanistic insight can point toward new ways to target disease—gives the work a real sense of purpose. Put simply: turning “what if?” into “here’s how” has been the most exhilarating part of my career so far.
Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why?
I’d be an NMR spectrometer—quiet, patient, and obsessed with tiny motions. It listens to the whispers of atoms, reveals hidden conformations and dynamics, and turns noisy, mysterious signals into stories about how proteins breathe, bind, and switch. It’s perfect for someone who delights in seeing the unseen: sensitive to subtle allostery, versatile across systems, and endlessly curious.

Health Trump administration’s drug strategy is at odds with recent actions on funding, policy Hard line on drug cartels, mixed signals on fentanyl test strips and other harm reduction Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article PABLO PORCIUNCULA/AFP via Getty Images By Lev Facher May 6, 2026 Addiction Reporter Lev Facher lev.facher@statnews.com Lev Facher covers the U.S. addiction and overdose crisis. Get email alerts for Lev Facher Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts The White House’s new strategy for addressing the nation’s drug crisis calls for a number of consensus public health measures: the overdose-reversal medication naloxone, medication-assisted treatment, and test strips used to detect fentanyl or other drug supply adulterants. But the May 4 document appears to run counter to many of the Trump administration’s latest drug policy actions. In particular, it comes just days after the administration issued new restrictions on using federal dollars to distribute test strips and warned against the use of medication-assisted treatment unless accompanied by other services, like counseling. Advertisement The document, issued each year by the White House Office of National Drug Control Policy, is the first to be released since the Senate confirmed Sara Carter , a former Fox News correspondent, to the role in January. addiction , chronic diseases , opioids , White House Submit a correction request Reprints Lev Facher Addiction Reporter Lev Facher covers the U.S. addiction and overdose crisis. The White House’s new strategy for addressing the nation’s drug crisis calls for a number of consensus public health measures: the overdose-reversal medication naloxone, medication-assisted treatment, and test strips used to detect fentanyl or other drug supply adulterants. But the May 4 document appears to run counter to many of the Trump administration’s latest drug policy actions. In particular, it comes just days after the administration issued new restrictions on using federal dollars to distribute test strips and warned against the use of medication-assisted treatment unless accompanied by other services, like counseling. Advertisement The document, issued each year by the White House Office of National Drug Control Policy, is the first to be released since the Senate confirmed Sara Carter , a former Fox News correspondent, to the role in January.

Health Why the FDA is authorizing fruit-flavored vapes Public health experts are divided about a decision that came after reported White House pressure Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Sarah Todd and Lizzy Lawrence May 6, 2026 Sarah Todd sarah.todd@statnews.com Sarah Todd returned to reporting in January 2025 after being assignment editor at STAT since October 2022. You can reach Sarah on Signal at sarahlizchar.47. She was previously a medical devices reporter. You can reach Lizzy on Signal at lizzylaw.53. Get email alerts for Lizzy Lawrence Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts WASHINGTON — The Food and Drug Administration authorized fruit-flavored vapes for the first time on Tuesday, after reports of pressure from President Trump. The approval might sound like a move guaranteed to alarm public health experts. But the decision is instead proving divisive among researchers and anti-smoking advocates as they debate whether the potential benefits in helping people quit cigarettes outweigh the risks of youth uptake. Advertisement They do, however, share concerns about reports that politics tainted the scientific process. WASHINGTON — The Food and Drug Administration authorized fruit-flavored vapes for the first time on Tuesday, after reports of pressure from President Trump. The approval might sound like a move guaranteed to alarm public health experts. But the decision is instead proving divisive among researchers and anti-smoking advocates as they debate whether the potential benefits in helping people quit cigarettes outweigh the risks of youth uptake. Advertisement They do, however, share concerns about reports that politics tainted the scientific process. Political appointees have become increasingly involved in making scientific decisions across the agency .

articles.title: HHS tackles ‘overmedication'
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Newsletter Morning Rounds HHS tackles ‘overmedication’ The cruise ship hantavirus outbreak, 988 staffing problems, and more health news from Morning Rounds Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images By Theresa Gaffney May 6, 2026 Morning Rounds Writer and Reporter Theresa Gaffney theresa.gaffney@statnews.com Theresa Gaffney is the lead Morning Rounds writer and reports on health care, new research, and public policy, with a particular interest in mental health, gender-affirming care, and LGBTQ+ patient communities. You can reach Theresa on Signal at theresagaff.97. Get email alerts for Theresa Gaffney Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Get your daily dose of health and medicine every weekday with STAT’s free newsletter Morning Rounds. Sign up here . Good morning. Are you keeping up with “The Odyssey”? I’m right on track, six books in. Athena’s working hard for her boys. Advertisement Amid hantavirus outbreak, cruise ship will move ports The cruise ship moored off the coast of Cabo Verde because of a hantavirus outbreak may soon be on the move. Spanish authorities told the BBC on Tuesday that the ship will go to the Canary Islands, which are part of Spain. Earlier in the day Maria Van Kerkhove, WHO’s director of epidemic and pandemic management, signaled that a move was in the works, with the goal being to get the ship to a location where there is more scientific support for the effort to determine whether there was transmission aboard the ship, and how the outbreak started. Three people from the ship who are suspected of being infected will be evacuated to the Netherlands, home base of the cruise liner’s owner, before the ship departs Cabo Verde, Van Kerkhove said. health care Submit a correction request Reprints Theresa Gaffney Morning Rounds Writer and Reporter Theresa Gaffney is the lead Morning Rounds writer and reports on health care, new research, and public policy, with a particular interest in mental health, gender-affirming care, and LGBTQ+ patient communities. You can reach Theresa on Signal at theresagaff.97. Amid hantavirus outbreak, cruise ship will move ports
The cruise ship moored off the coast of Cabo Verde because of a hantavirus outbreak may soon be on the move. Spanish authorities told the BBC on Tuesday that the ship will go to the Canary Islands, which are part of Spain. Earlier in the day Maria Van Kerkhove, WHO’s director of epidemic and pandemic management, signaled that a move was in the works, with the goal being to get the ship to a location where there is more scientific support for the effort to determine whether there was transmission aboard the ship, and how the outbreak started. Three people from the ship who are suspected of being infected will be evacuated to the Netherlands, home base of the cruise liner’s owner, before the ship departs Cabo Verde, Van Kerkhove said.

articles.title: Researchers urge study of paternal deaths, though a new paper finds fatherhood is protective
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Health Researchers urge study of paternal deaths, though a new paper finds fatherhood is protective Public health experts take issue with framing of paternal mortality as a crisis Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Camille MacMillin/STAT By Annalisa Merelli May 4, 2026 Contributing Writer Annalisa Merelli Annalisa.Merelli@statnews.com Annalisa (Nalis) Merelli is a contributing writer at STAT focused on boys’ and men’s health. Get email alerts for Annalisa Merelli Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Maternal health is a known crisis in the U.S., where pregnant women and new mothers die at a rate several times higher than in comparable countries. In recent years, increased awareness of the problem has led to interventions at the federal and state level and a strengthening of surveillance and data collection. Even as sizable improvements continue to be elusive, the picture of how many new mothers are dying, and why, is becoming clearer. A research letter published on Monday in JAMA Pediatrics argues fathers deserve similar attention. To bolster their assertion, the authors reported the results of a pilot study in Georgia of deaths among fathers of children born in a single year, which found nearly 800 deaths in the first five years of fatherhood. Advertisement “It’s been more common in my experience that there’s a dad who has died during the course of the mom’s either pregnancy or in the postnatal period,” Craig Garfield, a  professor of pediatrics at Northwestern University and the lead author of the study, said in an interview. Maternal health is a known crisis in the U.S., where pregnant women and new mothers die at a rate several times higher than in comparable countries. In recent years, increased awareness of the problem has led to interventions at the federal and state level and a strengthening of surveillance and data collection. Even as sizable improvements continue to be elusive, the picture of how many new mothers are dying, and why, is becoming clearer.
several times higher
federal
state level
A research letter published on Monday in JAMA Pediatrics argues fathers deserve similar attention. To bolster their assertion, the authors reported the results of a pilot study in Georgia of deaths among fathers of children born in a single year, which found nearly 800 deaths in the first five years of fatherhood.
JAMA Pediatrics
Advertisement
“It’s been more common in my experience that there’s a dad who has died during the course of the mom’s either pregnancy or in the postnatal period,” Craig Garfield, a  professor of pediatrics at Northwestern University and the lead author of the study, said in an interview.

articles.title: OxyContin maker Purdue Pharma set to dissolve after judge approves its criminal sentence
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Health OxyContin maker Purdue Pharma set to dissolve after judge approves its criminal sentence ‘It is not lost on me that those who started the epidemic will not serve a sentence,’ the judge said Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Kathleen Scarpone, left, and others protest in front of the Arthur M. Sackler Museum at Harvard University in Cambridge, Mass., in 2019. Scarpone lost her son to OxyContin addiction. Josh Reynolds/AP By Associated Press April 30, 2026 NEWARK, N.J. — OxyContin maker Purdue Pharma is set to be dissolved and replaced by a company focused on the public good by the week’s end, as a massive legal settlement resolving thousands of lawsuits takes effect. A federal judge on Tuesday delivered a criminal sentence to the company to resolve a Department of Justice probe — a last necessary step to clear the way for the settlement. Advertisement U.S. District Judge Madeline Cox Arleo made her decision after listening to hours of impact statements from people who lost loved ones or struggled with addiction themselves and requested she reject the negotiated sentence. While she didn’t go that far, she said she sympathized with people who bore the brunt of an epidemic linked to more than 900,000 deaths in the U.S. since 1999. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. A federal judge on Tuesday delivered a criminal sentence to the company to resolve a Department of Justice probe — a last necessary step to clear the way for the settlement. Advertisement U.S. District Judge Madeline Cox Arleo made her decision after listening to hours of impact statements from people who lost loved ones or struggled with addiction themselves and requested she reject the negotiated sentence. While she didn’t go that far, she said she sympathized with people who bore the brunt of an epidemic linked to more than 900,000 deaths in the U.S. since 1999. articles.title: Then and Now: Robert Blelloch Embraces Serendipity in Stem Cell and Cancer Research

Skip to main content Article Then and Now: Robert Blelloch Embraces Serendipity in Stem Cell and Cancer Research From studying microRNAs in embryonic stem cells to exploring exosomes in anti-tumor immunity, Blelloch illustrates how discovery guides research. Written by Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga, PhD Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga, PhD Go to The Scientist's LinkedIn Page Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga is a Brazilian freelance science writer, who covers the quirky and fascinating biology of cells and animals. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . May 6, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Robert Blelloch explores how exosome proteins modulate anti-tumor immune responses. Image credit: Gregory Cowley Photography, Elisabeth Fall, Erin Lemieux PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share A cademic scientist Robert Blelloch ’s research has been in constant evolution since he decided to dedicate his life to science. In 2012, The Scientist interviewed Blelloch about the journey of his research career and early work on epigenetics in cell fate determination and cancer. 1-3 In celebration of The Scientist ’s 40 th anniversary, we reached out to Blelloch again to see what paths his scientific work has taken over the last 14 years. From exploring the roles of microRNAs in stem cells and as cancer biomarkers to uncovering key functions of exosome proteins in anti-tumor immunity, Blelloch’s work reveals a mindset that embraces serendipity and is unafraid to take risks to advance scientific discovery. 4-8 MicroRNAs: Molecular Regulators of Embryonic Stem Cell Proliferation and Pluripotency In the mid-2000s, as a freshly-minted group leader at the University of California, San Francisco, Blelloch focused on microRNAs —small, non-coding RNA molecules that regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally—and their effects on embryonic stem cells. Embryonic stem cells can differentiate into any cell type in the human body. Without a differentiation signal, these cells self-renew, meaning they proliferate indefinitely without losing their undifferentiated features. To understand the role microRNAs play in embryonic stem cell proliferation and pluripotency, Blelloch’s team developed an embryonic stem cell knockout model for the DGCR8 cofactor, which forms a complex with the enzyme Drosha that is essential for microRNA maturation, and they showed that loss of DGCR8 led to defects in embryonic stem cell proliferation. 9 Using their newly developed Dgcr8 knockout system, they identified members of the miR-290 family that rescued the embryonic stem cell proliferation capacity. 4 Blelloch’s team also found that the same microRNA cluster promotes the dedifferentiation of somatic cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), suggesting that these small RNAs play a role not only in embryonic stem cell self-renewal but could also be used to generate iPSCs. 5 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe From Embryonic Stem Cells to Cancer Biomarkers While Blelloch recognized that his work on the post-transcriptional regulation of pluripotency established his team’s standing in that field, his desire to explore new scientific questions led him to pursue another path in the microRNA universe: their potential use as cancer biomarkers. The tumor microenvironment consists of a mix of tumor cells and different types of tumor-infiltrating immune cells. In his lab at the University of California, San Francisco, Robert Blelloch studies tumor immunology. Li Wang and Hannah Driks, Blelloch’s lab. Inspired by a student’s idea and emerging evidence of the presence of microRNAs in the plasma of patients with cancer, Blelloch examined whether specific microRNAs in the blood could serve as diagnostic and prognostic markers in people with prostate cancer. 10 By developing a method that enabled the detection of even small amounts of microRNAs, his team found that specific microRNAs in the serum were associated with prostate cancer progression. What’s more, adding these small RNAs into the Cancer of the Prostate Risk Assessment (CAPRA) model improved cancer risk prediction, supporting the use of these molecules in clinical settings. 6,7 The discovery of microRNAs circulating in the blood led Blelloch and his team to question how exactly these molecules moved inside the human body. One possibility was that extracellular vesicles , particularly in the form of exosomes, carried them around. “Vesicles are basically lipid packages, and they can transfer material from one cell to another,” Blelloch explained. “Does that mean microRNAs are being transferred from one cell to another in the blood?” Blelloch’s team set out to investigate this question, but it turned out to be a fruitless pursuit. “After three years it was kind of a failure,” he recalled. “[The] poor graduate student working on it was looking for evidence of microRNA transfer via these vesicles, and at least in our hands, we were seeing very little evidence for significant transfer.” The setback sent the researchers back to the drawing board, where they started rethinking the possible roles these exosomes might have. Long-Distance Control of the Anti-Tumor Immune Response Exosomes derive from the plasma membrane of cells, meaning they are covered with insoluble proteins that can act as molecular signals, even over long distances. This idea prompted Blelloch and his team to shift their focus from microRNAs to proteins. They focused on the programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-L1), a molecule known to suppress the anti-tumor immune response, Blelloch explained. Using genetic knockouts of key enzymes involved in exosome biosynthesis, the researchers showed that tumors secrete PD-L1-packed exosomes that travel away from the tumor microenvironment to the lymph nodes, where PD-L1 inhibits T cell activity. 8 Blelloch emphasized that advanced gene-editing technologies, such as CRISPR, were imperative for bringing this work to life. “[In] the paper, everything's CRISPR-ed out. I could've never done that in 2012. No way,” he said. Continue reading below... Product Thermo Scientific™ Multiskan™ Ease Absorbance Microplate Reader Absorbance, redefined. Multiskan™ Ease delivers fast, reproducible results with minimal training. LED optics remove lamp changes, while cloud-enabled SkanIt™ software simplifies setup and analysis for reliable ELISA workflows. Read More While their findings suggested that PD-L1 plays a key role in exosome-mediated immunosuppression, Blelloch explained that some results indicated that the protein does not act alone. Using mass spectrometry in the tumor-derived exosomes, his team recently identified the poliovirus receptor-related 2 (PVRL2) molecule, a protein believed to act as an immune checkpoint protein, as another suppressor of the anti-tumor immune response. 11 Blelloch and his team are further exploring PVRL2’s role in cancer immunology. By letting discoveries guide his research, Blelloch’s scientific journey reveals how researchers can chase the most exciting results while making significant contributions to their fields. “The fun of being an academic is just let things flow; let things take you where they take you and hope for the best,” he said. “The downside of this kind of career is [that] it’s very risk-taking [and] very insecure. You get beaten down constantly, but boy, there is a lot of joy.” References Hochedlinger K, et al. Reprogramming of a melanoma genome by nuclear transplantation . Genes Dev . 2004;18(15):1875-1885. Blelloch RH, et al. Nuclear cloning of embryonal carcinoma cells . Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A . 2004;101(39):13985-13990. Blelloch R, et al. Reprogramming efficiency following somatic cell nuclear transfer is influenced by the differentiation and methylation state of the donor nucleus . Stem Cells . 2006;24(9):2007-2013. Wang Y, et al. Embryonic stem cell-specific microRNAs regulate the G1-S transition and promote rapid proliferation . Nat Genet . 2008;40(12):1478-1483. Judson RL, et al. Embryonic stem cell-specific microRNAs promote induced pluripotency . Nat Biotechnol . 2009;27(5):459-461. Moltzahn F, et al. Microfluidic-based multiplex qRT-PCR identifies diagnostic and prognostic microRNA signatures in the sera of prostate cancer patients . Cancer Res . 2011;71(2):550-560. Wang SY, et al. miR-19, miR-345, miR-519c-5p serum levels predict adverse pathology in prostate cancer patients eligible for active surveillance . PLoS One . 2014;9(6):e98597. Published 2014 Jun 3. Poggio M, et al. Suppression of Exosomal PD-L1 Induces Systemic Anti-tumor Immunity and Memory . Cell . 2019;177(2):414-427.e13. Wang Y, et al. DGCR8 is essential for microRNA biogenesis and silencing of embryonic stem cell self-renewal . Nat Genet . 2007;39(3):380-385. Mitchell PS, et al. Circulating microRNAs as stable blood-based markers for cancer detection . Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A . 2008;105(30):10513-10518. Yang J, et al. PVRL2 Suppresses Antitumor Immunity through PVRIG- and TIGIT-independent Pathways . Cancer Immunol Res . 2024;12(5):575-591. Related Topics anti-tumor immunity , Cancer , exosomes , Immunology , microRNAs , prostate cancer , stem cells Meet the Author Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga, PhD Go to The Scientist's LinkedIn Page Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga is a Brazilian freelance science writer, who covers the quirky and fascinating biology of cells and animals. She has a PhD in neuroscience from the Federal University of Sao Paulo and a specialized certificate in Science Communication from the University of California, San Diego. She has words in The Scientist, Drug Discovery News, The Montana Natural History Center, among others. View Full Profile Share You might also be interested in... eBook PCR Assays Capture Gene Expression Insights PCR-based techniques have transformed how scientists analyze transcripts’ translational applications in cancer and neuroscience research. Share The introduction of real-time quantitative PCR (qPCR) in the early 1990s opened the door for quantitative gene expression analysis. Still, qPCR depends on standard curves, allowing only relative quantification, and can be susceptible to variations in amplification efficiency. Digital PCR has emerged as a solution that provides highly sensitive absolute quantification of nucleic acids without the need for standard curves. Validation and Analysis of 12,000 AI-Driven CAR T Designs in the Bits to Binders Competition Examine how large-scale CAR T-cell functional validation informs the efficacy of AI-driven protein binder design methodologies. Designing Translational CRISPR Genome Editing A platform approach to gene editing brings together design, manufacturing, validation, analytics, and regulatory considerations right from the start. Automating Large-Scale Plasmid DNA Purification In this webinar, Lee Hoang will examine automated strategies for improving scalability and consistency in large-scale plasmid DNA purification. Robert Blelloch explores how exosome proteins modulate anti-tumor immune responses. Image credit: Gregory Cowley Photography, Elisabeth Fall, Erin Lemieux PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share A cademic scientist Robert Blelloch ’s research has been in constant evolution since he decided to dedicate his life to science. In 2012, The Scientist interviewed Blelloch about the journey of his research career and early work on epigenetics in cell fate determination and cancer. 1-3 In celebration of The Scientist ’s 40 th anniversary, we reached out to Blelloch again to see what paths his scientific work has taken over the last 14 years. From exploring the roles of microRNAs in stem cells and as cancer biomarkers to uncovering key functions of exosome proteins in anti-tumor immunity, Blelloch’s work reveals a mindset that embraces serendipity and is unafraid to take risks to advance scientific discovery. 4-8 MicroRNAs: Molecular Regulators of Embryonic Stem Cell Proliferation and Pluripotency In the mid-2000s, as a freshly-minted group leader at the University of California, San Francisco, Blelloch focused on microRNAs —small, non-coding RNA molecules that regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally—and their effects on embryonic stem cells. Embryonic stem cells can differentiate into any cell type in the human body. Without a differentiation signal, these cells self-renew, meaning they proliferate indefinitely without losing their undifferentiated features. To understand the role microRNAs play in embryonic stem cell proliferation and pluripotency, Blelloch’s team developed an embryonic stem cell knockout model for the DGCR8 cofactor, which forms a complex with the enzyme Drosha that is essential for microRNA maturation, and they showed that loss of DGCR8 led to defects in embryonic stem cell proliferation. 9 Using their newly developed Dgcr8 knockout system, they identified members of the miR-290 family that rescued the embryonic stem cell proliferation capacity. 4 Blelloch’s team also found that the same microRNA cluster promotes the dedifferentiation of somatic cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), suggesting that these small RNAs play a role not only in embryonic stem cell self-renewal but could also be used to generate iPSCs. 5 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe From Embryonic Stem Cells to Cancer Biomarkers While Blelloch recognized that his work on the post-transcriptional regulation of pluripotency established his team’s standing in that field, his desire to explore new scientific questions led him to pursue another path in the microRNA universe: their potential use as cancer biomarkers. The tumor microenvironment consists of a mix of tumor cells and different types of tumor-infiltrating immune cells. In his lab at the University of California, San Francisco, Robert Blelloch studies tumor immunology. Li Wang and Hannah Driks, Blelloch’s lab. Inspired by a student’s idea and emerging evidence of the presence of microRNAs in the plasma of patients with cancer, Blelloch examined whether specific microRNAs in the blood could serve as diagnostic and prognostic markers in people with prostate cancer. 10 By developing a method that enabled the detection of even small amounts of microRNAs, his team found that specific microRNAs in the serum were associated with prostate cancer progression. What’s more, adding these small RNAs into the Cancer of the Prostate Risk Assessment (CAPRA) model improved cancer risk prediction, supporting the use of these molecules in clinical settings. 6,7 The discovery of microRNAs circulating in the blood led Blelloch and his team to question how exactly these molecules moved inside the human body. One possibility was that extracellular vesicles , particularly in the form of exosomes, carried them around. “Vesicles are basically lipid packages, and they can transfer material from one cell to another,” Blelloch explained. “Does that mean microRNAs are being transferred from one cell to another in the blood?” Blelloch’s team set out to investigate this question, but it turned out to be a fruitless pursuit. “After three years it was kind of a failure,” he recalled. “[The] poor graduate student working on it was looking for evidence of microRNA transfer via these vesicles, and at least in our hands, we were seeing very little evidence for significant transfer.” The setback sent the researchers back to the drawing board, where they started rethinking the possible roles these exosomes might have. Long-Distance Control of the Anti-Tumor Immune Response Exosomes derive from the plasma membrane of cells, meaning they are covered with insoluble proteins that can act as molecular signals, even over long distances. This idea prompted Blelloch and his team to shift their focus from microRNAs to proteins. They focused on the programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-L1), a molecule known to suppress the anti-tumor immune response, Blelloch explained. Using genetic knockouts of key enzymes involved in exosome biosynthesis, the researchers showed that tumors secrete PD-L1-packed exosomes that travel away from the tumor microenvironment to the lymph nodes, where PD-L1 inhibits T cell activity. 8 Blelloch emphasized that advanced gene-editing technologies, such as CRISPR, were imperative for bringing this work to life. “[In] the paper, everything's CRISPR-ed out. I could've never done that in 2012. No way,” he said. Continue reading below... Product Thermo Scientific™ Multiskan™ Ease Absorbance Microplate Reader Absorbance, redefined. Multiskan™ Ease delivers fast, reproducible results with minimal training. LED optics remove lamp changes, while cloud-enabled SkanIt™ software simplifies setup and analysis for reliable ELISA workflows. Read More While their findings suggested that PD-L1 plays a key role in exosome-mediated immunosuppression, Blelloch explained that some results indicated that the protein does not act alone. Using mass spectrometry in the tumor-derived exosomes, his team recently identified the poliovirus receptor-related 2 (PVRL2) molecule, a protein believed to act as an immune checkpoint protein, as another suppressor of the anti-tumor immune response. 11 Blelloch and his team are further exploring PVRL2’s role in cancer immunology. By letting discoveries guide his research, Blelloch’s scientific journey reveals how researchers can chase the most exciting results while making significant contributions to their fields. “The fun of being an academic is just let things flow; let things take you where they take you and hope for the best,” he said. “The downside of this kind of career is [that] it’s very risk-taking [and] very insecure. You get beaten down constantly, but boy, there is a lot of joy.” References Hochedlinger K, et al. Reprogramming of a melanoma genome by nuclear transplantation . Genes Dev . 2004;18(15):1875-1885. Blelloch’s team set out to investigate this question, but it turned out to be a fruitless pursuit. “After three years it was kind of a failure,” he recalled. “[The] poor graduate student working on it was looking for evidence of microRNA transfer via these vesicles, and at least in our hands, we were seeing very little evidence for significant transfer.”

Share The introduction of real-time quantitative PCR (qPCR) in the early 1990s opened the door for quantitative gene expression analysis. Still, qPCR depends on standard curves, allowing only relative quantification, and can be susceptible to variations in amplification efficiency. Digital PCR has emerged as a solution that provides highly sensitive absolute quantification of nucleic acids without the need for standard curves. articles.title: Researchers Reflect on the Lasting Impact of Science’s Landmark Discoveries
articles.text:
Skip to main content Article Researchers Reflect on the Lasting Impact of Science’s Landmark Discoveries From PCR and CRISPR to autophagy and targeted cancer therapies, scientists share the discoveries that shaped their work and continue to influence the future of research. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . May 2, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Researchers share landmark discoveries that inspire them. Image credit: © iStock.com , Natalya Kosarevich PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share S cience is a series of milestones, from fundamental discoveries to innovative technologies. Each one acts like a building block, supporting the next breakthrough and paving the way for future generations of researchers. To explore the personal impact of these milestones, we asked our readers: Which landmark scientific discovery or invention do you find most inspiring, and why? Cell Biology: From Autophagy to Immortal Cells Kaamini Dhanabalan is a bioengineer and molecular imaging scientist. Kaamini Dhanabalan Kaamini Dhanabalan , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara I find the discovery of autophagy deeply inspiring. The idea that cells have an internal recycling system, one that breaks down damaged components and repurposes them for survival, completely changed how we think about health and aging. It shifted the narrative from cells passively accumulating damage to cells actively maintaining themselves. The significance of this work was recognized globally when the 2016 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for uncovering the mechanisms of autophagy, which underscored how a fundamental cellular process can reshape medicine. What fascinates me most is how central autophagy is across biological processes and diseases like neurodegeneration, cancer, immunity, metabolism, and aging. During my PhD, studying autophagy closely showed me how tightly regulated and context-dependent it is. Now seeing intermittent fasting enter mainstream conversations as a way to stimulate autophagy is striking. A molecular pathway once limited to research labs is now influencing everyday health decisions and remains my exact motivation to bridge benches and beds. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Khyati Raina’s work combines stem cell biology and organoid technology. Khyati Raina Khyati Raina , a postdoctoral fellow at Albert Einstein College of Medicine Two discoveries have really shaped how I think about science: the development of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. The idea behind iPSCs is fascinating. It’s almost like reversing time at the cellular level. Scientists can take a fully developed cell, like a skin fibroblast, and reprogram it back into a pluripotent state by changing the set of proteins it expresses. From there, it has the potential to become almost any cell type in the body. The fact that you can generate cells so closely related to an individual, starting from their own tissue, completely changed how we study development and disease. CRISPR-Cas9, on the other hand, feels like having a highly precise molecular tool. It allows us to target and edit specific regions of DNA with remarkable accuracy. Together, these technologies have transformed research across many fields and continue to open up possibilities that once seemed out of reach. Molecular Biology: Amplifying and Silencing Genes Aditya Gupta is a parasitologist at USDA-ARS. Aditya Gupta Aditya Gupta , a postdoctoral fellow at the US Department of Agriculture-Agricultural Research Service (USDA-ARS) The invention of the polymerase chain reaction ( PCR ) by Kary Mullis is particularly inspiring to me. I read this story during my doctoral research, and I still remember how he ran back to his lab to check the PCR results. PCR transformed biology by allowing researchers to amplify tiny amounts of DNA and detect organisms that would otherwise remain hidden. In my work, PCR-based methods are essential for identifying parasites in meat samples and studying their genetic diversity. This technology illustrates how a single innovation can revolutionize multiple fields, from diagnostics to evolutionary biology, and enable discoveries that directly impact public health and disease surveillance. Kasturika Shankar is a biochemist and biophysicist who studies host-pathogen interactions. Kasturika Shankar Kasturika Shankar , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Buffalo The discovery of RNA interference, the finding that cells have a built-in system for silencing specific genes using small RNA molecules. Researchers stumbled upon it almost by accident, and yet it revolutionized both our understanding of gene regulation and gave scientists a powerful tool with which to study and potentially treat disease. It's a reminder that curiosity-driven, fundamental research can lead somewhere transformative in ways nobody initially anticipated. Health Science: From Precision Oncology to Harm Reduction Neil Carleton studies the tumor biology of age-related breast cancers. Neil Carleton Neil Carleton , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Pittsburgh The field of breast cancer research has been particularly inspiring to follow, both historically and in the modern era of precision oncology. The discovery of the estrogen receptor and the development of tamoxifen were among the earliest and most influential examples of targeted cancer therapy. These advances laid the groundwork for an entire field focused on endocrine-targeted treatments. Similarly, the identification of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) and the development of trastuzumab dramatically transformed outcomes for patients with HER2-positive breast cancer. Looking back at these milestones has been especially exciting, and they continue to inform how we think about precision medicine today. Mia Rough is a behavioral neuropharmacologist at Wake Forest University School of Medicine. Mia Rough Mia Rough , a postdoctoral fellow at Wake Forest University School of Medicine One of the developments I find most inspiring is the emergence of harm reduction tools such as naloxone (Narcan) and fentanyl test strips. What inspires me about these advances is that they reflect a public health approach grounded in compassion and evidence, where the immediate goal is to save lives. Harm reduction recognizes that people deserve care and protection regardless of where they are in recovery. To me, some of the most important advances in substance use science are the ones that reduce suffering in the present while also creating opportunities for better long-term outcomes. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage. Related Topics Cell & Molecular Biology , CRISPR , drug discovery , drugs , PCR , Public Health , technology Share You might also be interested in... Article Understanding Long-Read cDNA Sequencing for Rare Genetic Disease Long-read sequencing addresses the limitations of short-read RNA-seq and can capture isoform-specific changes. Share Short-read RNA-seq provides robust gene-level expression but is limited in resolving full-length transcripts and splice variants. Long-read sequencing addresses these limitations by capturing complete isoforms, enabling direct interrogation of transcript structure and isoform-specific changes. This information can identify biomarkers and support therapeutic development. Download this case study to learn more about how full-length transcript sequencing can detect treatment-associated splice variants and characterize transcriptome-wide expression changes. Sponsored by Top Image Credit: ©iStock, filo You might also be interested in... Interview Postdoc Portrait: Kaustav Gangopadhyay By studying the conformational dynamics of kinases, one postdoc reveals how tiny structural changes govern major cellular responses. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Nov 17, 2025 | 2 min read Save for Later This postdoc studies how tiny shifts in kinase structure steer the cell’s larger responses. Image credit: St. Jude Children's research Hospital, ©iStock, selvanegra PDF version Share Q | Write a brief introduction to yourself including the lab you work in and your research background. I am Kaustav Gangopadhyay , a postdoctoral research associate in structural biology at St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital with Dr. Babis Kalodimos. My research explores understanding the conformational dynamics of kinases. I earned my PhD in Biological Sciences from IISER Kolkata, where my work centered on the T cell kinase ZAP-70 and its role in antigen discrimination through kinetic proofreading. Q | How did you first get interested in science and/or your field of research? My interest in science began early with a fascination for how invisible processes shape the visible world around us. I was particularly intrigued by the question of how cells make decisions with such precision, and how mistakes in these decisions can lead to disease. This curiosity grew into a passion for molecular biology and biochemistry during my undergraduate training, where I realized that structural biology offers a unique window into life at atomic detail—allowing us to see not just what proteins do, but how they do it. Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite research directions has been exploring the structural and mechanistic regulation of kinases, enzymes that act as central hubs in cellular signaling. What excites me about studying kinases is their dual nature—they are both highly conserved in their catalytic machinery and incredibly diverse in their regulatory strategies. This balance makes them ideal systems to probe fundamental principles of allostery, cooperativity, and signaling fidelity. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe During my PhD, I focused on the immune kinase ZAP-70, dissecting how its tandem SH2 domains enforce kinetic proofreading in T cells, ensuring discrimination between self and non-self-antigens. Now, in my postdoctoral work, I try to understand the conformational dynamics of kinases owing to understand the toggling of states between inactive and active conformation. Q | What do you find most exciting about your research project? The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been the moments when abstract questions about cellular behavior collapse into clear, testable molecular stories. There’s a special thrill in collecting noisy biophysical data, watching peaks move on an NMR spectrum or a kinetic trace change shape, and then realizing those signals map onto a concrete structural mechanism that explains how a kinase or receptor actually works. Equally exciting has been the collaborative and mentoring side of science: designing experiments with colleagues across disciplines, training juniors to think critically about controls and interpretation, and watching them grow into independent scientists. Finally, the translational edge—knowing that mechanistic insight can point toward new ways to target disease—gives the work a real sense of purpose. Put simply: turning “what if?” into “here’s how” has been the most exhilarating part of my career so far. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I’d be an NMR spectrometer—quiet, patient, and obsessed with tiny motions. It listens to the whispers of atoms, reveals hidden conformations and dynamics, and turns noisy, mysterious signals into stories about how proteins breathe, bind, and switch. It’s perfect for someone who delights in seeing the unseen: sensitive to subtle allostery, versatile across systems, and endlessly curious. Article Researchers Reflect on the Lasting Impact of Science’s Landmark Discoveries From PCR and CRISPR to autophagy and targeted cancer therapies, scientists share the discoveries that shaped their work and continue to influence the future of research. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . May 2, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Researchers share landmark discoveries that inspire them. Image credit: © iStock.com , Natalya Kosarevich PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share S cience is a series of milestones, from fundamental discoveries to innovative technologies. Each one acts like a building block, supporting the next breakthrough and paving the way for future generations of researchers. To explore the personal impact of these milestones, we asked our readers: Which landmark scientific discovery or invention do you find most inspiring, and why? Cell Biology: From Autophagy to Immortal Cells Kaamini Dhanabalan is a bioengineer and molecular imaging scientist. Kaamini Dhanabalan Kaamini Dhanabalan , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara I find the discovery of autophagy deeply inspiring. The idea that cells have an internal recycling system, one that breaks down damaged components and repurposes them for survival, completely changed how we think about health and aging. It shifted the narrative from cells passively accumulating damage to cells actively maintaining themselves. The significance of this work was recognized globally when the 2016 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for uncovering the mechanisms of autophagy, which underscored how a fundamental cellular process can reshape medicine. What fascinates me most is how central autophagy is across biological processes and diseases like neurodegeneration, cancer, immunity, metabolism, and aging. During my PhD, studying autophagy closely showed me how tightly regulated and context-dependent it is. Now seeing intermittent fasting enter mainstream conversations as a way to stimulate autophagy is striking. A molecular pathway once limited to research labs is now influencing everyday health decisions and remains my exact motivation to bridge benches and beds. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Khyati Raina’s work combines stem cell biology and organoid technology. Khyati Raina Khyati Raina , a postdoctoral fellow at Albert Einstein College of Medicine Two discoveries have really shaped how I think about science: the development of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. The idea behind iPSCs is fascinating. It’s almost like reversing time at the cellular level. Scientists can take a fully developed cell, like a skin fibroblast, and reprogram it back into a pluripotent state by changing the set of proteins it expresses. From there, it has the potential to become almost any cell type in the body. The fact that you can generate cells so closely related to an individual, starting from their own tissue, completely changed how we study development and disease. CRISPR-Cas9, on the other hand, feels like having a highly precise molecular tool. It allows us to target and edit specific regions of DNA with remarkable accuracy. Together, these technologies have transformed research across many fields and continue to open up possibilities that once seemed out of reach. Molecular Biology: Amplifying and Silencing Genes Aditya Gupta is a parasitologist at USDA-ARS. Aditya Gupta Aditya Gupta , a postdoctoral fellow at the US Department of Agriculture-Agricultural Research Service (USDA-ARS) The invention of the polymerase chain reaction ( PCR ) by Kary Mullis is particularly inspiring to me. I read this story during my doctoral research, and I still remember how he ran back to his lab to check the PCR results. PCR transformed biology by allowing researchers to amplify tiny amounts of DNA and detect organisms that would otherwise remain hidden. In my work, PCR-based methods are essential for identifying parasites in meat samples and studying their genetic diversity. This technology illustrates how a single innovation can revolutionize multiple fields, from diagnostics to evolutionary biology, and enable discoveries that directly impact public health and disease surveillance. Kasturika Shankar is a biochemist and biophysicist who studies host-pathogen interactions. Kasturika Shankar Kasturika Shankar , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Buffalo The discovery of RNA interference, the finding that cells have a built-in system for silencing specific genes using small RNA molecules. Researchers stumbled upon it almost by accident, and yet it revolutionized both our understanding of gene regulation and gave scientists a powerful tool with which to study and potentially treat disease. It's a reminder that curiosity-driven, fundamental research can lead somewhere transformative in ways nobody initially anticipated. Health Science: From Precision Oncology to Harm Reduction Neil Carleton studies the tumor biology of age-related breast cancers. Neil Carleton Neil Carleton , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Pittsburgh The field of breast cancer research has been particularly inspiring to follow, both historically and in the modern era of precision oncology. The discovery of the estrogen receptor and the development of tamoxifen were among the earliest and most influential examples of targeted cancer therapy. These advances laid the groundwork for an entire field focused on endocrine-targeted treatments. Similarly, the identification of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) and the development of trastuzumab dramatically transformed outcomes for patients with HER2-positive breast cancer. Looking back at these milestones has been especially exciting, and they continue to inform how we think about precision medicine today. Mia Rough is a behavioral neuropharmacologist at Wake Forest University School of Medicine. Mia Rough Mia Rough , a postdoctoral fellow at Wake Forest University School of Medicine One of the developments I find most inspiring is the emergence of harm reduction tools such as naloxone (Narcan) and fentanyl test strips. What inspires me about these advances is that they reflect a public health approach grounded in compassion and evidence, where the immediate goal is to save lives. Harm reduction recognizes that people deserve care and protection regardless of where they are in recovery. To me, some of the most important advances in substance use science are the ones that reduce suffering in the present while also creating opportunities for better long-term outcomes. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage.
