Article Understanding Long-Read cDNA Sequencing for Rare Genetic Disease Long-read sequencing addresses the limitations of short-read RNA-seq and can capture isoform-specific changes. Share Short-read RNA-seq provides robust gene-level expression but is limited in resolving full-length transcripts and splice variants. Long-read sequencing addresses these limitations by capturing complete isoforms, enabling direct interrogation of transcript structure and isoform-specific changes. This information can identify biomarkers and support therapeutic development. Download this case study to learn more about how full-length transcript sequencing can detect treatment-associated splice variants and characterize transcriptome-wide expression changes. Aditya Gupta Aditya Gupta , a postdoctoral fellow at the US Department of Agriculture-Agricultural Research Service (USDA-ARS) The invention of the polymerase chain reaction ( PCR ) by Kary Mullis is particularly inspiring to me. I read this story during my doctoral research, and I still remember how he ran back to his lab to check the PCR results. PCR transformed biology by allowing researchers to amplify tiny amounts of DNA and detect organisms that would otherwise remain hidden. In my work, PCR-based methods are essential for identifying parasites in meat samples and studying their genetic diversity. This technology illustrates how a single innovation can revolutionize multiple fields, from diagnostics to evolutionary biology, and enable discoveries that directly impact public health and disease surveillance. Kasturika Shankar is a biochemist and biophysicist who studies host-pathogen interactions. Kasturika Shankar Kasturika Shankar , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Buffalo The discovery of RNA interference, the finding that cells have a built-in system for silencing specific genes using small RNA molecules. Researchers stumbled upon it almost by accident, and yet it revolutionized both our understanding of gene regulation and gave scientists a powerful tool with which to study and potentially treat disease. It's a reminder that curiosity-driven, fundamental research can lead somewhere transformative in ways nobody initially anticipated. Health Science: From Precision Oncology to Harm Reduction Neil Carleton studies the tumor biology of age-related breast cancers. Neil Carleton Neil Carleton , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Pittsburgh The field of breast cancer research has been particularly inspiring to follow, both historically and in the modern era of precision oncology. The discovery of the estrogen receptor and the development of tamoxifen were among the earliest and most influential examples of targeted cancer therapy. These advances laid the groundwork for an entire field focused on endocrine-targeted treatments. Similarly, the identification of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) and the development of trastuzumab dramatically transformed outcomes for patients with HER2-positive breast cancer. Looking back at these milestones has been especially exciting, and they continue to inform how we think about precision medicine today. Mia Rough is a behavioral neuropharmacologist at Wake Forest University School of Medicine. Mia Rough Mia Rough , a postdoctoral fellow at Wake Forest University School of Medicine One of the developments I find most inspiring is the emergence of harm reduction tools such as naloxone (Narcan) and fentanyl test strips. What inspires me about these advances is that they reflect a public health approach grounded in compassion and evidence, where the immediate goal is to save lives. Harm reduction recognizes that people deserve care and protection regardless of where they are in recovery. To me, some of the most important advances in substance use science are the ones that reduce suffering in the present while also creating opportunities for better long-term outcomes. Responses have been edited for length and clarity.
S cience is a series of milestones, from fundamental discoveries to innovative technologies. Each one acts like a building block, supporting the next breakthrough and paving the way for future generations of researchers. To explore the personal impact of these milestones, we asked our readers: Which landmark scientific discovery or invention do you find most inspiring, and why?
S
Cell Biology: From Autophagy to Immortal Cells
Kaamini Dhanabalan is a bioengineer and molecular imaging scientist. Kaamini Dhanabalan
Kaamini Dhanabalan is a bioengineer and molecular imaging scientist.
Kaamini Dhanabalan
Kaamini Dhanabalan , a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, Santa Barbara
I find the discovery of autophagy deeply inspiring. The idea that cells have an internal recycling system, one that breaks down damaged components and repurposes them for survival, completely changed how we think about health and aging. It shifted the narrative from cells passively accumulating damage to cells actively maintaining themselves. The significance of this work was recognized globally when the 2016 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for uncovering the mechanisms of autophagy, which underscored how a fundamental cellular process can reshape medicine. What fascinates me most is how central autophagy is across biological processes and diseases like neurodegeneration, cancer, immunity, metabolism, and aging. During my PhD, studying autophagy closely showed me how tightly regulated and context-dependent it is. Now seeing intermittent fasting enter mainstream conversations as a way to stimulate autophagy is striking. A molecular pathway once limited to research labs is now influencing everyday health decisions and remains my exact motivation to bridge benches and beds.
autophagy

Khyati Raina
Khyati Raina , a postdoctoral fellow at Albert Einstein College of Medicine
Two discoveries have really shaped how I think about science: the development of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. The idea behind iPSCs is fascinating. It’s almost like reversing time at the cellular level. Scientists can take a fully developed cell, like a skin fibroblast, and reprogram it back into a pluripotent state by changing the set of proteins it expresses. From there, it has the potential to become almost any cell type in the body. The fact that you can generate cells so closely related to an individual, starting from their own tissue, completely changed how we study development and disease. CRISPR-Cas9, on the other hand, feels like having a highly precise molecular tool. It allows us to target and edit specific regions of DNA with remarkable accuracy. Together, these technologies have transformed research across many fields and continue to open up possibilities that once seemed out of reach.

Share Short-read RNA-seq provides robust gene-level expression but is limited in resolving full-length transcripts and splice variants. Long-read sequencing addresses these limitations by capturing complete isoforms, enabling direct interrogation of transcript structure and isoform-specific changes. This information can identify biomarkers and support therapeutic development. Interview Postdoc Portrait: Kaustav Gangopadhyay By studying the conformational dynamics of kinases, one postdoc reveals how tiny structural changes govern major cellular responses. Written by The Scientist The Scientist View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Nov 17, 2025 | 2 min read Save for Later This postdoc studies how tiny shifts in kinase structure steer the cell’s larger responses. Image credit: St. Jude Children's research Hospital, ©iStock, selvanegra PDF version Share Q | Write a brief introduction to yourself including the lab you work in and your research background. I am Kaustav Gangopadhyay , a postdoctoral research associate in structural biology at St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital with Dr. Babis Kalodimos. My research explores understanding the conformational dynamics of kinases. I earned my PhD in Biological Sciences from IISER Kolkata, where my work centered on the T cell kinase ZAP-70 and its role in antigen discrimination through kinetic proofreading. Q | How did you first get interested in science and/or your field of research? My interest in science began early with a fascination for how invisible processes shape the visible world around us. I was particularly intrigued by the question of how cells make decisions with such precision, and how mistakes in these decisions can lead to disease. This curiosity grew into a passion for molecular biology and biochemistry during my undergraduate training, where I realized that structural biology offers a unique window into life at atomic detail—allowing us to see not just what proteins do, but how they do it. Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite research directions has been exploring the structural and mechanistic regulation of kinases, enzymes that act as central hubs in cellular signaling. What excites me about studying kinases is their dual nature—they are both highly conserved in their catalytic machinery and incredibly diverse in their regulatory strategies. This balance makes them ideal systems to probe fundamental principles of allostery, cooperativity, and signaling fidelity. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe During my PhD, I focused on the immune kinase ZAP-70, dissecting how its tandem SH2 domains enforce kinetic proofreading in T cells, ensuring discrimination between self and non-self-antigens. Now, in my postdoctoral work, I try to understand the conformational dynamics of kinases owing to understand the toggling of states between inactive and active conformation. Q | What do you find most exciting about your research project? The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been the moments when abstract questions about cellular behavior collapse into clear, testable molecular stories. There’s a special thrill in collecting noisy biophysical data, watching peaks move on an NMR spectrum or a kinetic trace change shape, and then realizing those signals map onto a concrete structural mechanism that explains how a kinase or receptor actually works. Equally exciting has been the collaborative and mentoring side of science: designing experiments with colleagues across disciplines, training juniors to think critically about controls and interpretation, and watching them grow into independent scientists. Finally, the translational edge—knowing that mechanistic insight can point toward new ways to target disease—gives the work a real sense of purpose. Put simply: turning “what if?” into “here’s how” has been the most exhilarating part of my career so far. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I’d be an NMR spectrometer—quiet, patient, and obsessed with tiny motions. It listens to the whispers of atoms, reveals hidden conformations and dynamics, and turns noisy, mysterious signals into stories about how proteins breathe, bind, and switch. It’s perfect for someone who delights in seeing the unseen: sensitive to subtle allostery, versatile across systems, and endlessly curious.
